feedback.text: I would like to start off by thanking the commission for this initiative. I am a student in game development in the Netherlands, so some of the topics that the Digital Fairness Act addresses are ones that I deeply care about. Within creative fields, we as artists and designers have a very unique connection to our consumers. They are passionate about our works, and we share that very same passion in our own work. However, because of executive decisions outside of our control, the end product could end up as a vastly worse experience. A game with dark patterns and addictive design elements impedes the passion of consumers, and with it the passion of us artists who are creating the product. This lack of satisfaction in our own creations contributes to burnout, which is rampant in the industry. These sorts of profit-oriented decisions also erode the trust between consumers and game developers, further magnifying this issue. By obstructing these design elements, the Digital Fairness Act helps improve the overall working conditions of game developers. However, there is one concern that I have that goes unaddressed in the DFA. As digital products, video games sometimes depend on the online services of developers to remain functional. When a video game relies heavily on these online services, and the developer decides to end those services, every working copy of the game could become entirely inaccessible to play. This is different from other digital products, such as downloadable music or films. When bought and retrieved, their access to the consumer is not beholden to the actions of the distributors, but this cannot be said for video games. I wish to ask the commission to expand the consumer protections in the DFA to address this practice. This might be done by making game developers leave any of their games in an accessible state after ending their services, or by making developers provide consumers with sufficient resources to substitute the loss of the developer's online services. I think the Digital Fairness Act will do much good in the name of consumer protection, and I hope that the commission will consider my feedback so that it might do a little bit more. Thank you for the improvements that you bring to the field of game development and our working conditions, even circumstantially. Best regards.
